1.3: three days of walking , that is to say, it took three days to walk through the streets and announce the Lord's threats.
1.16: . ― Joppé , today Jaffa, a seaport on the Mediterranean, west of Jerusalem.
1.4: . Jonah in the belly of the fish is a figure of Jesus Christ in the tomb. Cf. Matthew ,
1.40: . — We are dealing here with a truly miraculous narrative. Why seek a purely supernatural action from natural agents? God, who saved Jonah, could easily have found in his wisdom a way to accomplish his will. But this way is impossible for us to know. This miracle currently leads the majority of exegetes to deny any historical basis to the Book of Jonah. It is impossible, they say, for a man to live three days and three nights without air. This argument only holds water for those who do not believe in miracles. Jesus himself declared this when he told the Jews that they would have no other miracle than that of Jonah (cf. Matthew

2.1: And the Lord brought a great fish to swallow Jonah, and Jonah was in the belly of the fish three days and three nights.
2.2: And from the belly of the fish, Jonah prayed to the Lord his God.
2.3: He said: «From my distress I called upon the Lord and he answered me; from the belly of Sheol I cried out: you have heard my voice.
2.4: You threw me into the abyss, into the heart of the seas, and the currents surrounded me. All your waves and all your surges swept over me.
2.5: And I said: I am driven from before your eyes, yet I will still behold your holy temple.
2.6: The waters had enveloped me to the soul, the abyss surrounded me, seaweed encircled my head.
2.7: I had descended to the roots of the mountains, the earth's locks were drawn upon me forever, and you brought my life up from the pit, Lord, my God.
2.8: When my soul fainted within me, I remembered the Lord and my prayer reached you, to your holy temple.
2.9: Those who cling to futile vanities abandon the author of their grace.
2.10: But I, with praise, will offer you a sacrifice; the vow I have made, I will fulfill. Salvation belongs to the Lord.
2.11: The Lord spoke to the fish, and the fish vomited Jonah onto the earth.

3.1: The word of the Lord came to Jonah a second time, saying:
3.2: «Get up, go to Nineveh, that great city, and preach to it the message I will tell you.»
3.3: And Jonah arose and went to Nineveh according to the word of the Lord. Now Nineveh was a great city in God's sight, three days' journey away.
3.4: Jonah began to enter the city, a day's walk, and he preached, saying, "In forty days Nineveh will be destroyed."«
3.5: The people of Nineveh believed in God, they proclaimed a fast, and they put on sackcloth, from the greatest to the least.
3.6: When the news reached the king of Nineveh, he rose from his throne, took off his robe, covered himself with sackcloth, and sat down in ashes.
3.7: And a cry went out in Nineveh, and by decree of the king and his nobles, these words were spoken: «Neither man nor beast, oxen nor sheep, shall taste anything, nor feed, nor drink water,
3.8: Let them cover themselves with sackcloth, both men and animals, let them cry out to God with strength, and let each one turn away from his evil ways and from the violent acts that his hands commit.
3.9: Who knows if God will not repent and turn from his fierce anger, so that we do not perish?»
3.10: God saw what they were doing, how they turned away from their evil ways, and God relented from the punishment with which he had threatened them.

4.1: Jonah was deeply grieved and angered.
4.2: He prayed to the Lord and said, «Ah, Lord, is this not what I said when I was still in my own country? That is why I first fled to Tarshish, for I knew that you are a merciful and gracious God, slow to anger, abounding in grace, and relenting from sending calamity.
4.3: Now, Lord, take my soul from me, for it is better for me to die than to live.»
4.4: And the Lord answered, «Do you have a right to be angry?»
4.5: And Jonah went out of the city and sat down to the east of the city, there he made himself a shelter and sat under it in the shade until he saw what would happen in the city.
4.6: And the Lord God made a castor oil plant grow up over Jonah to give shade over his head, to deliver him from his suffering, and Jonah was very glad because of the castor oil plant.
4.7: But the Lord brought a worm at daybreak the next day, which attacked the castor oil plant and it withered.
4.8: And when the sun rose, the Lord brought a scorching wind from the east, and the sun beat down on Jonah's head, so that he fainted. He asked to die and said, "It is better for me to die than to live."«
4.9: Then God said to Jonah, «Do you have a right to be angry about the castor oil plant?» He replied, «It is right for me to be angry, even to the point of death.»
4.10: And the Lord said, «You grieve over the castor oil plant, for which you did not labor and which you did not make grow, which came up in a night and perished in a night,
4.11: And I would not grieve over Nineveh, that great city, in which there are more than
